original_abstract: The chemical diversity of natural antioxidants (AOXs) makes it difficult to separate, detect, and quantify individual antioxidants from a complex food/biological matrix. Moreover, the total antioxidant power is often more meaningful to evaluate health beneficial effects because of the cooperative action of individual antioxidant species. Currently, there is no single antioxidant assay for food labeling because of the lack of standard quantification methods. Antioxidant assays may be broadly classified as the electron transfer (ET)- and hydrogen atom transfer (HAT)-based assays. The results obtained are hardly comparable because of the different mechanisms, redox potentials, pH and solvent dependencies, etc. of various assays. This project will aid the identification and quantification of properties and mutual effects of antioxidants, bring a more rational basis to the classification of antioxidant assays with their constraints and challenges, and make the results more comparable and understandable. In this regard, the task group members convey their own experiences in various methods of antioxidants measurement.
humanized_abstract: The wide variation in chemical structures and diverse biological functions of natural antioxidants (AOXs) have made it very difficult to accurately separate, identify and quantify the individual antioxidants present in a mixed food or biological matrix. Because of their mutual synergies, the total antioxidant activity is always more useful and effective in confirming the positive health effects of mixed food components. Unfortunately, no single reliable assay for antioxidant determination has yet been agreed upon as a standard for food labelling. Various antioxidant assays are now categorized mainly into two types of assays based on the principle of electron transfer (ET)- and hydrogen atom transfer (HAT)-based assays. Because of great differences in redox potentials, pH and effect of different solvents in different assays, the validity and reliability of the results obtained from the different methods are therefore not comparable. In this perspective, this IUPAC project will facilitate the exploration of new information concerning the identification and assessment of activities and interactions of AOXs, the more scientific basis and evaluation for the limitations and potentials of individual antioxidant assays and for making the antioxidant activity data for food and food ingredients more comparable and reliable. Each member of this task group will briefly introduce their personal experiences concerning various antioxidant assays.
